result:
:::writing
There is a particular kind of quiet that arrives just before something changes. It isn’t silence, not exactly. It’s more like the world holding its breath, as if every object has agreed, just for a moment, not to move unless absolutely necessary. You can feel it in places that shouldn’t feel like anything at all—door handles, window glass, the smooth, indifferent surface of a kitchen counter. Even your own hands seem to hesitate, as though they are waiting for instructions that haven’t been written yet.

I noticed it first on a Tuesday, which is the most unremarkable day of the week and therefore the most likely to hide something important. Mondays carry too much expectation, Fridays too much relief. But Tuesday—Tuesday slips by unnoticed, like a stranger in a crowd who could be anyone. I was standing at the sink, rinsing a mug I didn’t remember using, when the quiet settled in.

The water kept running, but it sounded distant, like it was happening in another room. Outside, a car passed, its tires whispering against the pavement. Then nothing. Not even the refrigerator hummed. I remember thinking, This is it. Not in a dramatic way, not like the end of the world. More like the beginning of a sentence I hadn’t learned how to finish.

I turned off the tap and waited.

Nothing happened.

And yet, something had clearly begun.

The rest of the day unfolded as usual, which is to say, not at all as usual. People spoke to me, and I responded. Emails arrived, and I answered them. A bird landed on the railing outside my window and tilted its head as if it were trying to understand me. But underneath all of it, the quiet remained, like a second layer of reality pressed just beneath the first.

I started noticing small inconsistencies. The clock on my wall ticked, but the second hand seemed to hesitate before each movement, like it needed permission to continue. My phone buzzed with a notification that disappeared before I could read it. When I looked at my reflection in the bathroom mirror, it lagged for a fraction of a second, just long enough to make me question which version of me was the original.

None of it was enough to alarm anyone else. If I had tried to explain it, I would have sounded like someone searching for patterns in static. So I kept it to myself, carrying the quiet with me like a secret I didn’t fully understand.

That night, I dreamed of a room I had never seen before. It was empty except for a single chair placed in the center. The walls were white, but not the kind of white you get from paint. It was deeper than that, like the absence of color rather than its presence. There were no windows, no doors—at least, none that I could see.

I stood at the edge of the room, uncertain whether I was supposed to enter or simply observe. The chair faced away from me, as if someone had been sitting there and had just stepped out for a moment. I had the strange, unshakable feeling that if I walked around to the front of the chair, I would find someone still sitting in it.

Or worse, I would find that the chair was waiting for me.

I woke up before I could decide.

The quiet was still there in the morning, patient and unhurried. It didn’t demand my attention, but it didn’t let me forget it either. It was like living beside a river you couldn’t see but could always hear, even when you tried to ignore it.

Days passed. Or maybe it was the same day, repeating itself with slight variations, like a song played on a loop with a subtle distortion each time. I started to test things, gently at first. I would leave a book on the table and come back later to see if it had moved. It hadn’t, but sometimes it felt like it had considered it.

I spoke out loud when I was alone, just to hear my own voice. It sounded normal, but the words seemed to linger in the air longer than they should, as if they were reluctant to disappear.

“Is something happening?” I asked once, half-expecting an answer.

The quiet didn’t respond. But it felt… attentive.

Eventually, I stopped trying to understand it. Not because I wasn’t curious, but because curiosity implies there’s something to discover, and I wasn’t sure that was true anymore. The quiet didn’t feel like a puzzle. It felt like a presence, something that existed alongside everything else without needing to explain itself.

Then, one afternoon, it changed.

I was sitting by the window, watching people move along the street below. They walked with purpose, or pretended to. Some checked their phones, others stared straight ahead, all of them wrapped in their own small worlds. For a moment, I felt separate from them, like I was watching a film instead of real life.

And then the quiet deepened.

It wasn’t just beneath things anymore. It was everywhere, filling the space between seconds, stretching time thin until it almost disappeared. The people outside slowed, their movements becoming deliberate, almost careful. A man mid-step held his foot in the air for just a fraction too long. A woman brushing her hair paused, her hand suspended as if caught in an invisible current.

I realized, with a clarity that didn’t feel like my own, that the world wasn’t stopping.

It was waiting.

For what, I didn’t know. For me? That seemed unlikely, and yet there I was, sitting at the edge of something I couldn’t see, feeling as though I had been placed there for a reason I hadn’t been told.

I stood up.

The room responded—not visibly, not in any way I could point to, but in the same way a conversation changes when someone decides to speak. The air felt different, charged with a quiet expectation.

I took a step forward.

Somewhere, something shifted.

Not dramatically. Not with a crash or a flash of light. Just a small adjustment, like a piece of a puzzle sliding into place. The kind of movement you might miss if you weren’t paying attention.

But I was paying attention.

For the first time, I understood that the quiet hadn’t been leading up to this moment.

This moment had been creating the quiet.

And whatever came next wasn’t something that would happen to me.

It was something I would choose.

I stood there, in a room that felt both entirely familiar and completely unknown, aware that the world—my world, at least—had narrowed down to a single, fragile point.

A decision, waiting to be made.

I opened my mouth to speak, not because I knew what to say, but because silence, for the first time, felt like the wrong answer.

And just before the first word formed, just before the moment tipped into whatever came next, I felt the quiet shift again—not disappearing, not ending, but transforming into something else.

Something that sounded, unmistakably, like the beginning of a voice.

Not mine.

But close enough that I wasn’t sure it mattered.
:::writing

:::writing

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
